text: Which OOP principle allows a class to inherit from multiple interfaces?
subject: OOPs
explanation: Languages like Java/C# don't support multiple class inheritance but allow implementing multiple interfaces.

text: What is polymorphism?
options: Same interface, different implementations based on object type
subject: OOPs
explanation: Polymorphism allows objects of different classes to respond differently to the same method call.

text: What is encapsulation?
options: Bundling data and methods that operate on that data within a single unit
subject: OOPs
explanation: Encapsulation bundles data (attributes) and methods (functions) in a class, restricting direct access to internal state.

text: What is the difference between method overloading and method overriding?
options: Overloading: same name different params in same class; Overriding: redefine parent method in child class
subject: OOPs
explanation: Overloading: compile-time, same class, different signatures. Overriding: runtime, subclass redefines parent's method.

text: What is an abstract class?
options: A class with only static methods
A class that cannot be instantiated and may contain abstract methods
subject: OOPs
explanation: Abstract classes define a template with abstract methods (no body) that subclasses must implement. Cannot be instantiated directly.

text: What is the 'this' keyword in OOP?
options: A reference to the current instance of the class
subject: OOPs
explanation: 'this' (or 'self' in Python) refers to the current object instance, used to access its attributes and methods.

text: What is a constructor?
options: A special method called when an object is created to initialize it
subject: OOPs
explanation: Constructors initialize object state during creation. They have the same name as the class (or __init__ in Python).

text: What is the diamond problem in multiple inheritance?
options: Ambiguity when a class inherits from two classes that both inherit from a common base
subject: OOPs
explanation: The diamond problem creates ambiguity about which parent's method to call. Solved by interfaces (Java) or virtual inheritance (C++).

text: What is the Liskov Substitution Principle?
options: Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types without altering program correctness
subject: OOPs
explanation: LSP ensures subclass objects can replace parent objects without breaking functionality — the 'L' in SOLID.

text: What is the difference between an interface and an abstract class?
options: Interface has no implementation, abstract class can have partial implementation
subject: OOPs
explanation: Interfaces define contracts (pure method signatures). Abstract classes can have both abstract and concrete methods.

text: What is a destructor?
options: A method called when an object is destroyed to clean up resources
subject: OOPs
explanation: Destructors release resources (memory, file handles) when an object is destroyed. Called ~ClassName() in C++, __del__ in Python.

text: What is the difference between composition and inheritance?
subject: OOPs
explanation: Inheritance: Dog 'is-a' Animal. Composition: Car 'has-a' Engine. Composition is often preferred for flexibility.

text: What is a static method?
options: A method that can't be changed
A method that belongs to the class rather than any instance
subject: OOPs
explanation: Static methods belong to the class itself, called without creating an instance. They can't access instance variables.

text: What are access modifiers?
options: Keywords that set access levels for classes, methods, and variables (public, private, protected)
subject: OOPs
explanation: Public: accessible everywhere. Private: only within class. Protected: within class and subclasses.

text: What is the Open/Closed Principle?
options: Files should be opened then closed
Classes should be open for extension but closed for modification
subject: OOPs
explanation: OCP says you should be able to add new behavior by extending classes (inheritance/composition) without modifying existing code.

text: What is a virtual function?
options: A function declared in base class that can be overridden in derived classes for runtime polymorphism
subject: OOPs
explanation: Virtual functions enable runtime polymorphism — the correct derived class method is called based on actual object type.

text: What is the Singleton design pattern?
options: A pattern that ensures a class has exactly one instance with global access
subject: OOPs
explanation: Singleton restricts class instantiation to one object. Used for loggers, configurations, connection pools.

text: What is dependency injection?
options: Providing dependencies to objects from outside rather than creating them internally
subject: OOPs
explanation: DI provides dependencies externally (via constructor/setter/interface), promoting loose coupling and testability.

text: What is the Factory design pattern?
options: Creates objects without specifying the exact class, using a factory method
subject: OOPs
explanation: Factory pattern delegates object creation to factory methods, decoupling client code from concrete classes.

text: What is the difference between deep copy and shallow copy?
options: Shallow copy copies references, deep copy creates independent copies of nested objects
subject: OOPs
explanation: Shallow: copies references (shared nested objects). Deep: recursively copies all nested objects (independent clone).

text: What is the Observer design pattern?
options: A pattern where objects subscribe to and get notified about state changes in another object
subject: OOPs
explanation: Observer defines a one-to-many dependency — when the subject changes state, all observers are notified. Used in event systems.

text: What is early binding vs late binding?
options: Early binding is only for Java
Early binding: compile-time method resolution; Late binding: runtime method resolution
subject: OOPs
explanation: Early/static binding resolves at compile time (overloading). Late/dynamic binding resolves at runtime (overriding with virtual functions).

text: What is the Single Responsibility Principle?
options: A class should have only one reason to change
subject: OOPs
explanation: SRP: each class should have one responsibility/reason to change. Keeps classes focused and maintainable.

text: What is the purpose of the 'final' keyword in Java?
subject: OOPs
explanation: final class: can't be subclassed. final method: can't be overridden. final variable: can't be reassigned.

text: What is the Strategy design pattern?
options: Defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each, makes them interchangeable at runtime
subject: OOPs
explanation: Strategy pattern lets you select an algorithm at runtime by encapsulating each in a separate class implementing a common interface.

text: What is an abstract method?
options: A method declared without implementation that must be overridden in subclasses
subject: OOPs
explanation: Abstract methods have no body — subclasses must provide their own implementation.

text: What is the difference between aggregation and composition?
options: Both are has-a; in composition the child can't exist without parent, in aggregation it can
subject: OOPs
explanation: Composition: strong ownership (room dies with house). Aggregation: weak association (student exists without university).

text: What is a friend function in C++?
options: A function granted access to a class's private and protected members despite not being a member
subject: OOPs
explanation: Friend functions are declared with the 'friend' keyword inside a class, allowing access to its private members.

text: What is the Decorator design pattern?
options: Dynamically adds behavior to objects without modifying their class
subject: OOPs
explanation: Decorator wraps objects to add new behavior dynamically. Like adding scrollbars to a window or compression to a stream.

text: What is operator overloading?
options: Defining custom behavior for operators (+, -, ==, etc.) when used with user-defined types
subject: OOPs
explanation: Operator overloading lets you define how operators work with your classes (e.g., adding two Vector objects with +).

text: What is the Interface Segregation Principle?
options: Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they don't use
subject: OOPs
explanation: ISP: split large interfaces into smaller, specific ones so clients only implement what they need.

text: What is the Dependency Inversion Principle?
options: High-level modules should depend on abstractions, not low-level modules
subject: OOPs
explanation: DIP: depend on abstractions (interfaces), not concrete implementations. Both high and low-level modules depend on abstractions.

text: What is type casting in OOP?
options: Converting an object from one type to another (upcasting or downcasting)
subject: OOPs
explanation: Upcasting: child→parent (safe, implicit). Downcasting: parent→child (requires explicit cast, can fail).

text: What is a pure virtual function in C++?
options: A virtual function with = 0 that has no implementation and forces subclasses to implement it
subject: OOPs
explanation: Pure virtual (= 0) makes the class abstract — it can't be instantiated and subclasses must override the function.

text: What is the MVC architecture?
options: Model-View-Controller: separates data, UI, and logic into three components
subject: OOPs
explanation: Model: data/logic. View: UI/presentation. Controller: handles input and coordinates Model-View. Promotes separation of concerns.

text: What is the Template Method pattern?
options: Defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base class, letting subclasses override specific steps
subject: OOPs
explanation: Template Method defines algorithm structure in base class with hook methods — subclasses customize specific steps.

text: What is the difference between an object and a class?
options: Class is a blueprint/template, object is a specific instance of that class
subject: OOPs
explanation: Class: defines structure and behavior (blueprint). Object: a concrete instance created from that blueprint.

text: What is cohesion in OOP?
options: How closely related the responsibilities of a single module/class are
Number of methods in a class
subject: OOPs
explanation: High cohesion means a class is focused on a single purpose. Low cohesion means it does too many unrelated things.

text: What is coupling in OOP?
options: The degree of interdependence between software modules
subject: OOPs
explanation: Low coupling is desirable — modules should be independent. Tight coupling makes changes ripple across the system.

text: What is an anonymous class?
options: A class with no name, defined inline, often used for one-time implementations
subject: OOPs
explanation: Anonymous classes are defined inline without a name — common for event handlers and simple interface implementations.

text: What is the Adapter design pattern?
options: Converts the interface of a class into another interface clients expect
subject: OOPs
explanation: Adapter wraps an existing class with a new interface, making incompatible classes work together.

text: What is method chaining?
options: Calling multiple methods sequentially on the same object by returning 'this'
subject: OOPs
explanation: Method chaining: each method returns 'this', allowing builder.setName('x').setAge(25).build(). Creates fluent APIs.

text: What is the Prototype design pattern?
options: Creates new objects by cloning existing instances rather than constructing from scratch
subject: OOPs
explanation: Prototype pattern clones existing objects to create new ones — useful when object creation is expensive.

text: What is the SOLID principle?
options: Five design principles: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion
subject: OOPs
explanation: SOLID principles guide OOP design for maintainable, extensible, loosely-coupled software.

text: What is the difference between association, aggregation, and composition?
options: Association: general relationship; Aggregation: weak ownership; Composition: strong ownership with lifecycle dependency
subject: OOPs
explanation: Association: knows-about. Aggregation: has-a (independent lifecycles). Composition: has-a (dependent lifecycle — part dies with whole).

text: What is a mixin?
options: A class that provides reusable behavior to other classes through multiple inheritance without being a primary base class
subject: OOPs
explanation: Mixins are utility classes that provide specific behavior to be mixed into other classes (e.g., Serializable, Comparable).

text: What is the Command design pattern?
options: Encapsulates a request as an object, enabling parameterization, queuing, and undo operations
subject: OOPs
explanation: Command pattern wraps requests in objects supporting undo, redo, queuing, and logging of operations.

text: What is the difference between a class variable and an instance variable?
options: Class variable: shared among all instances; Instance variable: unique to each object
subject: OOPs
explanation: Class variables (static) are shared by all instances. Instance variables belong to a specific object.

text: What is duck typing?
options: If an object has the right methods/properties, it's accepted regardless of its actual type
subject: OOPs
explanation: 'If it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it's a duck.' Focus on behavior rather than type — common in Python/JavaScript.

text: What is RTTI (Run-Time Type Information)?
options: Mechanism to determine the type of an object at runtime (e.g., typeof, instanceof)
subject: OOPs
explanation: RTTI (dynamic_cast, typeid in C++; instanceof in Java) checks or converts object types at runtime.

text: What is the Facade design pattern?
options: Provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem
subject: OOPs
explanation: Facade provides a simple, unified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem, reducing complexity for clients.

text: What is object serialization?
options: Converting an object's state to a byte stream for storage or transmission
subject: OOPs
explanation: Serialization converts objects to byte streams (JSON, binary, XML). Deserialization reconstructs objects from streams.

text: What is the difference between IS-A and HAS-A relationships?
options: IS-A: inheritance (Dog IS-A Animal); HAS-A: composition (Car HAS-A Engine)
subject: OOPs
explanation: IS-A = inheritance hierarchy. HAS-A = composition/containment. Choose based on the real-world relationship.

text: What is a getter and setter?
options: Methods that provide controlled access to private fields
subject: OOPs
explanation: Getters read private fields, setters modify them with validation — implementing encapsulation.

text: What is the Builder design pattern?
options: Separates construction of complex objects from their representation, allowing step-by-step creation
subject: OOPs
explanation: Builder constructs complex objects step-by-step (e.g., building a meal: burger, drink, side). Avoids telescoping constructors.

text: What is multiple dispatch?
options: Selecting method implementation based on the runtime types of multiple arguments
subject: OOPs
explanation: Multiple dispatch (supported in Julia, CLOS) chooses method based on all argument types, not just the receiver.

text: A payment processing system has PayPal, Stripe, and RazorPay integrations, each with different APIs. Adding a new gateway requires changes in 15 places. Which pattern fixes this?
options: Strategy pattern — define a PaymentGateway interface, implement per-gateway, inject as needed
subject: OOPs
explanation: Strategy pattern: define PaymentGateway interface with process(). Each gateway (PayPal, Stripe) implements it. Adding a new one = one new class.

text: A notification system sends alerts via Email, SMS, and Push. A new requirement adds WhatsApp and Slack. The existing NotificationService class has if-else chains for each channel. How to refactor?
options: Use Observer pattern — each channel subscribes to events and handles notifications independently
subject: OOPs
explanation: Observer pattern: each channel (Email, SMS, Push, WhatsApp) implements NotificationObserver. Adding channels doesn't modify the NotificationService.

text: A game engine has enemy types: Zombie, Vampire, Robot. Each has different attack and movement behaviors that can be combined freely. Using inheritance leads to a class explosion. What's the better design?
options: Composition with Strategy pattern — inject AttackBehavior and MoveBehavior interfaces
subject: OOPs
explanation: Favor composition over inheritance. Define AttackBehavior (melee, ranged) and MoveBehavior (walk, fly) interfaces. Inject combinations into enemy objects.

text: An e-commerce application calculates shipping costs differently for Standard, Express, and International orders. The calculation logic is embedded in a single method with nested if-else. What OOP principle does this violate?
options: Open/Closed Principle — should use polymorphism with a ShippingStrategy interface
subject: OOPs
explanation: Violates OCP: modifying the method for each new shipping type. Fix: ShippingCalculator interface with Standard, Express, International implementations.

text: A logging library is needed across the entire application. Multiple logger instances cause duplicate log entries and resource contention. Which pattern solves this?
options: Singleton pattern — single logger instance with thread-safe initialization
subject: OOPs
explanation: Singleton ensures one logger instance application-wide, preventing duplicate entries and managing the file handle consistently.

text: A restaurant ordering system has Order objects that can have various toppings, sides, and options added. Creating subclasses for each combination is impractical. Which pattern allows dynamic addition of features?
options: Decorator pattern — wrap the base order with topping/side decorators
subject: OOPs
explanation: Decorator: BaseOrder wrapped by CheeseDecorator, BaconDecorator, etc. Each adds to price/description dynamically.

text: A data processing pipeline transforms data through multiple steps: validate, clean, transform, aggregate. The steps vary per data source. How to design this for flexibility?
options: Template Method pattern — define pipeline skeleton in base class, override steps in subclasses
subject: OOPs
explanation: Template Method: BasePipeline.process() calls validate(), clean(), transform(), aggregate(). Each data source subclass overrides specific steps.

text: An application needs to support multiple database backends (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB). The business logic should not depend on the specific database chosen. Which principle and pattern apply?
options: Dependency Inversion Principle + Repository pattern — depend on abstract repository, inject concrete implementation
subject: OOPs
explanation: DIP: business logic depends on IRepository interface. Concrete MySQLRepo, PostgresRepo, MongoRepo are injected. Switching DB = change injection config.

text: A UI component library has Button, TextBox, and Checkbox that need to look different across Windows, Mac, and Linux themes. Which pattern avoids theme-specific subclasses exponentially growing?
options: Abstract Factory — create families of related theme-specific components
subject: OOPs
explanation: Abstract Factory: WindowsUIFactory, MacUIFactory each produce themed Button, TextBox, Checkbox. Adding a theme = one new factory class.

text: A chat application stores messages in memory. As users request message history, the app creates thousands of message objects consuming excessive memory. Many messages have the same sender/format. How to optimize?
options: Flyweight pattern — share common message structures, store unique data externally
subject: OOPs
explanation: Flyweight shares intrinsic state (sender name, format) across messages. Extrinsic state (timestamp, content) is stored per message.

text: A system sends API requests to third-party services. Tests are slow because they hit real APIs. How do you design the code for testability?
options: Use Dependency Injection with an interface — inject mock/stub implementations during testing
subject: OOPs
explanation: Define an interface (e.g., IPaymentService). In production, inject the real implementation. In tests, inject a mock that returns predefined responses.

text: What is a class hierarchy?
options: A tree structure of classes connected through inheritance relationships
subject: OOPs
explanation: Class hierarchy organizes classes in an inheritance tree — base class at root, derived classes as branches.

text: What is the difference between throw and throws in Java?
options: throw: actually throws an exception; throws: declares what exceptions a method might throw
subject: OOPs
explanation: throw new Exception() actually throws. throws IOException declares the method might throw it — for checked exceptions.

text: What is covariant return types?
options: Allowing a subclass to override a method with a more specific return type
subject: OOPs
explanation: Covariant return: if base method returns Animal, derived can return Dog (a subtype of Animal). Supported in Java/C++.

text: What is the Law of Demeter?
options: A design guideline: an object should only interact with its immediate friends, not friends of friends
subject: OOPs
explanation: Law of Demeter (principle of least knowledge): don't call methods on objects returned by other methods (avoid train wrecks like a.getB().getC().doSomething()).

text: What is a sealed class?
options: A class that restricts which classes can extend it, defined at compile time
subject: OOPs
explanation: Sealed classes (Kotlin, Java 17+) restrict inheritance to a defined set of subclasses, enabling exhaustive when/switch checks.

text: What is the Proxy design pattern?
options: An object that acts as a placeholder/surrogate for another object to control access
subject: OOPs
explanation: Proxy controls access to another object — used for lazy loading, access control, logging, or remote access.

text: What is the Mediator design pattern?
options: Defines an object that encapsulates how a set of objects interact, reducing direct coupling
subject: OOPs
explanation: Mediator centralizes complex communications between objects. Instead of each object knowing all others, they communicate through the mediator.

text: What is the difference between abstract class and concrete class?
options: Abstract: can't be instantiated, may have abstract methods; Concrete: can be instantiated, all methods implemented
subject: OOPs
explanation: Abstract classes serve as templates — concrete classes provide full implementations and can create objects.

text: Related to Inheritance - What is the difference between IS-A and HAS-A relationships?
options: IS-A: inheritance (Dog IS-A Animal); HAS-A: composition (Car HAS-A Engine)
subject: OOPs
explanation: IS-A = inheritance hierarchy. HAS-A = composition/containment. Choose based on the real-world relationship.

text: Related to SOLID Principles - An application needs to support multiple database backends (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB). The business logic should not depend on the specific database chosen. Which principle and pattern apply?
options: Dependency Inversion Principle + Repository pattern — depend on abstract repository, inject concrete implementation
subject: OOPs
explanation: DIP: business logic depends on IRepository interface. Concrete MySQLRepo, PostgresRepo, MongoRepo are injected. Switching DB = change injection config.

text: Related to Design Patterns - What is the Proxy design pattern?
options: An object that acts as a placeholder/surrogate for another object to control access
subject: OOPs
explanation: Proxy controls access to another object — used for lazy loading, access control, logging, or remote access.

text: Related to Encapsulation - What is a friend function in C++?
options: A function granted access to a class's private and protected members despite not being a member
subject: OOPs
explanation: Friend functions are declared with the 'friend' keyword inside a class, allowing access to its private members.

text: Related to Design Patterns - What is the Singleton design pattern?
options: A pattern that ensures a class has exactly one instance with global access
subject: OOPs
explanation: Singleton restricts class instantiation to one object. Used for loggers, configurations, connection pools.

text: Related to Polymorphism - What is polymorphism?
options: Same interface, different implementations based on object type
subject: OOPs
explanation: Polymorphism allows objects of different classes to respond differently to the same method call.

text: Related to Polymorphism - What is covariant return types?
options: Allowing a subclass to override a method with a more specific return type
subject: OOPs
explanation: Covariant return: if base method returns Animal, derived can return Dog (a subtype of Animal). Supported in Java/C++.

text: Related to SOLID Principles - An application needs to support multiple database backends (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB). The business logic should not depend on the specific database chosen. Which principle and pattern apply?
options: Dependency Inversion Principle + Repository pattern — depend on abstract repository, inject concrete implementation
subject: OOPs
explanation: DIP: business logic depends on IRepository interface. Concrete MySQLRepo, PostgresRepo, MongoRepo are injected. Switching DB = change injection config.

text: Related to Encapsulation - What is a destructor?
options: A method called when an object is destroyed to clean up resources
subject: OOPs
explanation: Destructors release resources (memory, file handles) when an object is destroyed. Called ~ClassName() in C++, __del__ in Python.

text: Related to Design Patterns - What is the Command design pattern?
options: Encapsulates a request as an object, enabling parameterization, queuing, and undo operations
subject: OOPs
explanation: Command pattern wraps requests in objects supporting undo, redo, queuing, and logging of operations.

text: Related to Encapsulation - What is a friend function in C++?
options: A function granted access to a class's private and protected members despite not being a member
subject: OOPs
explanation: Friend functions are declared with the 'friend' keyword inside a class, allowing access to its private members.

text: Related to Design Patterns - What is the Facade design pattern?
options: Provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem
subject: OOPs
explanation: Facade provides a simple, unified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem, reducing complexity for clients.

text: Related to Design Patterns - A logging library is needed across the entire application. Multiple logger instances cause duplicate log entries and resource contention. Which pattern solves this?
options: Singleton pattern — single logger instance with thread-safe initialization
subject: OOPs
explanation: Singleton ensures one logger instance application-wide, preventing duplicate entries and managing the file handle consistently.

text: Related to Polymorphism - What is a virtual function?
options: A function declared in base class that can be overridden in derived classes for runtime polymorphism
subject: OOPs
explanation: Virtual functions enable runtime polymorphism — the correct derived class method is called based on actual object type.

text: Related to Encapsulation - What is a constructor?
options: A special method called when an object is created to initialize it
subject: OOPs
explanation: Constructors initialize object state during creation. They have the same name as the class (or __init__ in Python).

text: Which SOLID principle states that a class should have one reason to change?
subject: OOPS

text: What design pattern ensures a class only has one instance?
subject: OOPS

text: Which feature of OOP allows an object to take many forms?
subject: OOPS

text: In UML, a dashed arrow with a hollow triangle head represents:
subject: OOPS

text: What happens to objects without references in Java?
subject: OOPS

text: In a try-catch-finally block, when is finally executed?
subject: OOPS

text: Which SOLID principle is violated when a subclass throws an exception for a method defined in its base class?
subject: OOPS

text: In Java, what is the impact of catching an Error (like OutOfMemoryError)?
options: It allows the application to recover safely
It is heavily discouraged as the JVM state might be unstable
subject: OOPS

text: What is the purpose of the Protected access modifier?
options: Visible only to the same class
Visible to classes in the same package and subclasses
Visible only to subclasses regardless of package
subject: OOPS

text: In C++, virtual destructors are required when:
options: You want to construct a derived object
You delete a derived class object through a base class pointer
subject: OOPS

text: Which design pattern is used to add new functionality to an existing object without altering its structure?
subject: OOPS

text: Which of the following describes the difference between an abstract class and an interface in Java 8+?
options: Abstract classes cannot have default methods
A class can implement multiple interfaces but extend only one abstract class
subject: OOPS
